params.text:
Sore skin and leakage

Recognising problems early is one of the most valuable things you do. Sore or broken skin around the stoma — redness, weeping, itching or raw areas — is common and is usually linked to leakage or a poorly fitting appliance. Repeated leakage, where output escapes under the baseplate, needs to be reported rather than simply patched over, because it damages the skin and undermines the seal.

  • Sore skin: redness, weeping, itching or broken areas.
  • Leakage: output escaping under the baseplate.
  • Report it: the stoma nurse can review the fit and products.

params.alt: Illustration for Skin and stoma problems

params.text:
Changes in the stoma itself

The stoma can change in ways that need reporting. A prolapse is when the stoma becomes longer and sticks out much more than usual. A retraction is the opposite, when it sinks below the level of the skin, which often causes leaks. Some bleeding from the surface when cleaning is normal, but heavier bleeding, bleeding that does not stop, or blood in the output should always be reported.

The most urgent change is colour. A healthy stoma is pink or red, so if it turns pale, grey, dusky, purple or black, this may mean its blood supply is affected, and you must get help urgently. Any of these changes — prolapse, retraction, unusual bleeding or a change in colour — is something you observe and report to the stoma nurse or registered nurse, never something you try to fix or push back yourself.

params.question: A person's stoma has turned from its normal pink-red colour to a dusky, dark purple. What should you do?

answers.text: Treat it as urgent and get help straight away, as a colour change can mean the blood supply is affected.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. A pale, grey, purple or black stoma may mean a poor blood supply and needs urgent help.

answers.text: Assume it is normal and simply note it for the next care review.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — a marked colour change is a warning sign that needs reporting urgently, not later.

answers.text: Rub the stoma to bring the colour back.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — you never rub or manipulate the stoma; get help urgently.

answers.text: Put extra tape over the pouch and carry on.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — taping over it does nothing for a colour change; this needs urgent clinical help.
